text.title: Referral Ledger — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `referral-ledger` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a ledger from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: Entries are append-only. Corrections are added as new entries that reference the prior entry's id; rows are not edited in place.
rule.rationale: Editable history loses the decision trail. Append-only preserves what was believed at decision time, which is what audit and retro need.

rule.statement: The ledger has a fixed column schema (id, ts, owner, hypothesis, evidence, outcome, next). Custom per-row columns are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Heterogeneous schemas destroy aggregability. A fixed schema makes the ledger queryable across months.

rule.statement: Every entry includes an evidence link (commit, ticket, dashboard, transcript). Entries without links are flagged at weekly review.
rule.rationale: Without evidence anchors entries become opinion. Evidence anchors are the difference between a learning artefact and a wishlist.

rule.statement: Ledger is reviewed at a fixed cadence (weekly or monthly) by a named owner. The review produces a digest + next-action.

rationale.source: OKR review practice; weekly business review (Bezos memo culture)

rule.rationale: Ledgers that no one reads decay into write-only logs. The review cadence is what extracts the signal.

rule.statement: Ledger entries older than the published retention horizon (e.g. 24 months) are archived to cold storage but not deleted.
rule.rationale: Long-tail learning lives in old entries. Deletion erases institutional memory; archival preserves it cheaply.
